note: CURATED, AI-ASSISTED, NOT mechanically derived and NOT legally reviewed. Produced by an exploratory pilot (2026-07-22): for each ORS chapter with 2+ agencies implementing it via OAR rules (the shared-authority set already computed in _meta/agency-graph.json), an LLM read the chapter's cited statute text plus every implementing rule's full text in one pass and was asked to identify CANDIDATE inconsistencies — never to assert that a conflict exists. Every candidate below carries the exact document IDs and quoted text it is based on; nothing here should be read as a legal finding. See BACKLOG.md "Visualization" for the method note and docs/mcp.md-style disclaimers used elsewhere in this repo. This file is a snapshot of one run over 12 chapters (of 245 in the full shared-authority set), not an exhaustive or automatically-refreshed corpus scan — rerunning the pilot over the same or different chapters could surface different candidates.
methodology: Per chapter: pull every ORS section cited as authority by an implementing OAR rule in that chapter, plus the full text of every one of those rules (source: _meta/graph.json implements/implemented_by edges). Give the whole cluster to an LLM in one pass (not pairwise) and ask for candidate inconsistencies with quotes and citations, near-misses that looked like conflicts but resolved as non-issues on careful reading, and any citation-metadata artifacts noticed along the way. 12 chapters piloted: 3 chosen for moderate agency count + high staleness lag (801, 819, 435 — zero candidates found), then 9 more chosen the same way after the first batch showed the selection criteria matters (215, 408, 270, 646, 169, 283, 291, 820, 813 — ~15 candidates found).

candidates.summary: DOJ's contract-review dollar thresholds are roughly double the statute's, and collapse the statute's 3-way category split into 2 categories.

documents.quote: The Attorney General shall approve for legal sufficiency all personal services contracts, all architectural and engineering services contracts and all information technology contracts calling for payment in excess of $75,000 ... all public contracts not subject to subsection (1) ... that provide for payment in excess of $100,000...

...Public Contract that is for or includes the acquisition of Information Technology that requests or provides for payment in excess of $150,000... Any Public Contract other than those described in subsection 1(a) above that requests or provides for payment in excess of $250,000...

candidates.note: ORS 291.047(3) authorizes the AG to impose rules 'necessary to carry out the provisions of this section,' but the quoted text doesn't on its face authorize raising or restructuring the dollar triggers. Possible the AG has separate unquoted statutory authority for this; possible the rule (last touched 2011) never caught up with the statute.

candidates.summary: Stale internal cross-reference to a rule number that doesn't exist in the corpus.

documents.quote: A state agency that fails to report a substantive program change in a timely manner as required under subsection (1) of 122-040-0020 above shall immediately report to the director.

candidates.note: No OAR 122-040-0020 exists; division 040 in this corpus contains only -0040, -0050, -0060. The matching deadline text is actually in OAR 122-040-0050(1). Reads as an orphaned cross-reference from a prior renumbering.

artifacts: OAR 137-045-0020 statutes_implemented includes "OL 1997" and "Ch. 869" — fragments of "Oregon Laws 1997, chapter 869" split into pseudo-citations.
OAR 461-190-0211 statutes_implemented includes federal CFR cites incorrectly prefixed "ORS" (e.g. "ORS 45 CFR 260.20").
OAR 813-003-0001 legal_authority includes a bare "ORS 183" (a whole chapter, not a section).

candidates.summary: The same OAR rule sets two different acreage caps for the identical trigger phrase, with no qualifying text distinguishing them.

documents.quote: Permanent features of a power generation facility shall not use, occupy, or cover more than 12 acres unless an exception is taken pursuant to ORS 197.732 and OAR chapter 660, division 4.
Permanent features of a power generation facility shall not use, occupy or cover more than 20 acres unless an exception is taken pursuant to ORS 197.732 and OAR chapter 660, division 4.

candidates.note: Both subsections use identical language and the same escape valve; neither text explains why the cap differs. Possibly each routes from a different table entry not visible in the rule text alone — needs agency/legal confirmation.

candidates.summary: Rule text contains a self-declared 1995 sunset clause but the document is catalogued status: current with no note.

documents.quote: The provisions of sections (1) and (2) of this rule are repealed on September 6, 1995.

candidates.note: May be intentional legacy retention for numbering continuity (common in OAR), but as catalogued it presents as a currently-binding requirement that facially expired 30 years ago.

artifacts: OAR 335-060-0060 statutes_implemented pulls in ORS 215.785 (a land-use statute) for a Social Security Number rule with no substantive land-use connection — likely a mis-cited section number, propagating a false implementer link into the ORS 215 authority graph.
OAR 603-080-0020 statutes_implemented lists "ORS 215.70" — truncated, almost certainly "215.705".
~10 OAR 660-006 rules carry "Ch. 792" / "ORS 1993 OL" fragments of a single Oregon Laws citation, not parseable as ORS section numbers.

candidates.summary: ODOT's public-notice rule requires less publication than the statute demands for the same value band, while DAS's rule implementing the same statute matches the statute exactly.

documents.quote: the state agency ... shall give the notice of the proposed sale not less than once a week for three successive weeks by publication in one or more newspapers...
must publish notice ... not less than once a week for three successive weeks.
For properties valued between $5,001 and $25,000 — Twice during the two-week period preceding the sale...

candidates.note: For the $5,001-$25,000 band, ODOT's schedule is fewer/shorter publications than the statute's flat standard. ORS 270.130 contains no visible ODOT carve-out.

candidates.summary: A DAS rule defines two statutory cross-reference terms differently than the statute's own definitions section, while a third parallel term in the same rule matches exactly.

documents.quote: 'Urban growth boundary' has the meaning given that term in ORS 197.015 ... 'Urban reserve' means any land designated as an urban reserve under ORS 197A.245.
'Urban growth boundary' has the meaning given that term in ORS 195.060 ... 'Urban reserve' means any land designated as an Urban reserve under ORS 195.145.

candidates.note: The third parallel term ('urban unincorporated community') is defined identically in both, citing ORS 197.015 — which makes these two divergences stand out. Whether ORS 195.060/195.145 carry equivalent definitions to 197.015/197A.245 is outside this corpus.

candidates.summary: A 1990 ODOT rule, unamended since, uses a one-year figure that doesn't map cleanly onto the statute's 2017-amended 18-month/12-month figures.

documents.quote: 18-month window before re-offering after notice; 12-month discontinuance trigger before the notice process must be repeated.
At any time during a period of one year after the auction date from which no satisfactory bid was received, the Department may sell the property at a private negotiated sale.

candidates.note: Rule predates the 2017 statutory amendment by 27 years with no visible update. Flagged as a staleness candidate — the concept mapping between rule and statute isn't precise enough to call this a direct numeric contradiction.

artifacts: OAR 141-067-0215 statutes_implemented contains "ORS 273.413 - RS 273.456" — missing the "O" in "ORS".
OAR 141-067-0310 mixes an OAR self-reference into a statute-citation field, with a typo ("141-167" for "141-067").

candidates.summary: A rule restates the statute's 'immediate family' definition but drops one of its four listed categories, for the same veterans' emergency-assistance program.

documents.quote: 'Immediate family' means a spouse, unremarried surviving spouse, child or stepchild.
'Immediate family' means a spouse, child or stepchild.

candidates.note: The rule (2013) postdates the statute's last amendment (2007), so this isn't explained by the rule predating a later statutory change. The same rule section correctly defers 'veteran' to the statute by reference but chose to restate 'immediate family' independently, narrowing it.

candidates.summary: A rule's grant cap is double the statute's, citing a 2026 bill the corpus's statute snapshot may not yet reflect.

documents.quote: Grants under the program: (a) May not exceed $5,000 per veteran...
total disbursement amounts will not exceed $10,000 per veteran, per lifetime.

candidates.note: If HB 4132 (2026) amended ORS 408.115(2)(a), this is a corpus-freshness artifact (statute snapshot lagging a newer law), not a live conflict. If it didn't, this is a rule exceeding its enabling statute's cap. Not resolved in-corpus; needs the bill text.

artifacts: OAR 274-010-0100 and OAR 274-012-0001 use "ORS 2013 HB 2633" / "2013 HB 2633" as if it were an ORS section number — it's a bill number.

OAR 274-035-0005 legal_authority cites "ORS 408.510" and "ORS 408.530" — this cluster's own ors-408.518 text notes 408.530 was renumbered to 408.395 in 1999; 408.510 doesn't appear to exist in the retrieved chapter at all.
Many OAR rule titles are truncated to single dangling words ("Who", "Lost,", "Additional").

candidates.summary: A civil-penalty escalation rule shows no sign of the statute's 2-year reset window between violations.

documents.quote: The director may impose civil penalties ... (b) Not more than $2,500 for a second violation within two years from the date of the first violation. (c) Not more than $10,000 for a third violation within two years from the date of the first violation.
Violations occurring after the time prescribed for the elimination of the violation shall be considered repeat violations.

candidates.note: The rule's dollar amounts never exceed the statute's caps (not in tension on the numbers), but its escalation-by-count logic carries no visible two-year reset. A second violation 3 years after the first should, per the statute, still be capped at the first-violation ceiling. Needs confirmation whether the reset is applied in unwritten agency practice.

artifacts: Several OAR 137-020 rules have a standalone "(4)" or "(1)(yy)" entry in legal_authority/statutes_implemented — a citation like "ORS 646.608(1)(u) & (4)" split across array entries, leaving a bare fragment as its own item.
OAR 603-111-0020 statutes_implemented: "ORS 646932" — missing the period.

candidates.summary: Two rule regimes from different agencies and eras both claim to implement the same juvenile extended-detention statute, naming different approving bodies with different renewal cadences, with no cross-reference or supersession between them.

documents.citation: OAR 213-050-0060(1), Oregon Criminal Justice Commission, adopted 2003
documents.quote: A supervising authority ordered by the court to detain juveniles for an extended detention period pursuant to ORS 419C.453 must detain juveniles only in a detention facility which has an extended detention program plan approved by the JCPAC.

A supervising authority ordered by a court to detain a juvenile for a period of detention exceeding eight days pursuant to ORS 419C.453 must detain the juvenile in accordance with an extended detention program plan approved by the Youth Development Council.
The JCPAC may authorize the county to operate an extended detention program for a maximum of five years, subject to renewal of approval by JCPAC in the future.
An approved extended detention program plan must be renewed biennially ... [with] full Council reapproval every sixth year.

candidates.note: Both rule sets carry status: current with no supersedes link and no cross-reference to each other. Plausibly the 2019 rule reflects a real reorganization (juvenile-justice functions moving to the Youth Development Council) that quietly displaced the 2003 rule without a formal repeal being reflected in this corpus — but that reading isn't confirmed against ORS 419C.453 itself, which is outside this pilot's cluster.

artifacts: ors-169.090's title field reads "...juvenile detention facility operation TREATMENT OF PRISONERS" — a later section heading bled into this section's title during chapter-snapshot slicing.
Multiple OAR rule titles truncated mid-phrase ("Authority", "Modification,", "Suspension,").

chapters.note: No candidate inconsistencies found — the cleanest of the 12 chapters piloted. Main finding was a systematic rule-title truncation artifact (see artifacts), not a substantive issue.
artifacts: Systematic title-field truncation across ~15 OAR rules and one statute in this cluster (e.g. "Approval and" for "Approval and Execution of Financing Agreements"; ors-283.140's title is literally "and").
OAR 125-156-0000 legal_authority: ["2010 OL Ch. 30", "Sec. 13", "14"] — one citation incorrectly split into three array entries, with a bare orphaned "14".

candidates.summary: A 1977-vintage rule, never amended, cites an OAR section that no longer exists in the corpus — likely renumbered decades ago.

documents.quote: A bus equipped with the eight-light system shall comply with the standards as adopted by the State Board of Education, March 1975, OAR 581-053-0517(23)(k), Lamps and Signals.
cited by OAR 735-124-0030 but no document with this id exists in the corpus

candidates.note: No OAR 581-053-0517 exists in the corpus; division 581-053 runs 0002 through 0650 with no -0517. The current 'Lamps and Signals' rule is OAR 581-053-0240(41). Reads as a stale cross-reference never updated after a renumbering.

candidates.summary: Several DMV/ODOT rules haven't yet been updated for a statutory amendment defining 'organ transport vehicle,' operative 2027-01-01 — not yet a live conflict, but a forward-looking gap.

documents.quote: The amendments to 820.300 by section 17, chapter 278, Oregon Laws 2025, become operative January 1, 2027... Subject to conditions, limitations, prohibitions and penalties established for emergency vehicle, organ transport vehicle and ambulance drivers under ORS 820.320...
Emergency Vehicle [defined] only by reference to ORS 815.230, 816.310, 820.350, and 820.370 — no mention of 'organ transport vehicle'.

candidates.note: Not a present conflict (the amendment isn't operative yet), but these rules will need updating before 2027-01-01.

artifacts: OAR 581-053-0120 legal_authority renders one citation as "ORS820.100" (missing space).

OAR 581-053-0230(9)(q) cites ORS 820.180(1)(b) in its body text, but that citation never made it into the rule's structured legal_authority/statutes_implemented frontmatter fields — a graph-completeness gap.

candidates.summary: An ignition-interlock rule creates a second, undefined certificate track with no visible statutory basis.

documents.quote: The individual customer when there is a 6-month 'no negative certificate', or a 90-day 'no negative certificate.'
for the last 90 consecutive days [the sole certificate mechanism the statute recognizes for ending/continuing the IID requirement]

candidates.note: No statutory reference anywhere in this cluster to a '6-month' certificate, what triggers it, or its legal effect.

candidates.summary: Two OAR sections with near-identical substantive text are both marked current with no supersession link recorded between them.

documents.quote: accuracy test performance is within a range of 0.010 high to 0.020 low of the expected value.
accuracy test performance is within a range of 0.010 high to 0.020 low of the expected value.

candidates.note: The tolerance numbers agree (not a substantive conflict), but both are status: current with supersedes: [] and neither's history references the other — the kind of gap that could hide a real future divergence if only one is ever amended.

artifacts: OAR 812-002-0190 (Construction Contractors Board, about arbitration) lists ORS 813.460 (DUII license suspension) in statutes_implemented, with zero substantive connection — likely a mis-extraction, the strongest single citation-artifact flag in this pilot.
OAR 415-012-0057 legal_authority/statutes_implemented includes "ORS 430.01030" (not a valid section number) and cites "ORS 813.500", which does not appear to exist in ORS chapter 813.

chapters.note: First pilot chapter (of the original 3). No candidate inconsistencies found; Department of State Police rules in this cluster are narrowly scoped to field sobriety test procedure (delegated under ORS 801.272) and don't overlap DMV's registration/title rules.

First pilot chapter. No candidate inconsistencies found. One near-miss worth recording: a Parks & Recreation rule (OAR 736-010-0025) tows vehicles 'abandoned' in state parks after 72 hours, which looks like it could conflict with DMV's highway-abandonment criteria (no fixed hour count, different triggers under ORS 819.140) — on inspection these are not in tension: Parks & Rec exercises separate ORS 390 park authority and only borrows DMV's disposal procedure afterward.

First pilot chapter. No candidate inconsistencies found. A tiered civil-penalty schedule in OAR 333-505-0120 ($250/$500/$1,000) looked like it might diverge from ORS 435.256's $1,000 cap but is fully consistent (never exceeds the cap).
artifacts: OAR 410-200-0120 statutes_implemented/legal_authority contains malformed ORS citations that don't correspond to real sections (e.g. "ORS 435.1200", "ORS 435.3", "ORS 447.56") — likely citation-extraction artifacts.
